prompt:
Our importer fleet keeps blowing through partner API quotas: three teams call the same hosts, nobody honors the rate-limit headers the partners send back, and when a 429 lands everybody retries at once and makes it worse. I want one pacing gate all of them route through — rategate.ts, exporting class RateGate — and I want it boring and predictable. test_rategate.ts (node's built-in runner, scripted mock hosts on loopback) is the acceptance contract: `node --test test_rategate.ts` fully green, test file untouched. No npm packages; node builtins + global fetch only. We run node 26 with type stripping, so keep the TypeScript erasable — no enums, no namespaces, no parameter properties, and explicit .ts import extensions.

new RateGate(options) with these knobs, all optional: now (() => epoch ms, default Date.now), sleep ((ms) => Promise, default a real setTimeout), hostConcurrency (default 1), maxRetries (default 3), defaultRetryMs (default 1000). Every wait in the gate goes through the injected now/sleep pair — never a real timer or Date.now() lookup of its own.

gate.request(url, {method, headers, body}?) resolves to {status, body, headers} (headers as a plain object with lowercased names; body as text). Scheduling rules:

- Per host (host:port), requests dispatch strictly in the order request() was called, with at most hostConcurrency in flight. Different hosts never wait on each other.
- On a 429: work out the wait — Retry-After that is all digits means that many SECONDS; otherwise try it as an HTTP-date and wait until that instant per the injected clock; missing or unparseable falls back to defaultRetryMs. If the wait is positive, exactly ONE sleeper call for exactly that long; zero or negative means retry immediately with no sleeper call. The retried request goes again ahead of everything else queued for that host. At most maxRetries retries per request; when the budget is spent, hand the caller the final 429 response as-is.
- After a non-429 response whose X-RateLimit-Remaining is 0 and whose X-RateLimit-Reset parses as epoch seconds: hold that host's NEXT dispatch until the reset instant (again: one sleeper call when the wait is positive, none otherwise). Remaining above zero, or absent headers, means no pause. On a 429 the Retry-After rule above is the only thing that runs — never two sleeps for one response.
- Everything else — 2xx, 5xx, non-429 4xx — passes through untouched: no retry, no sleep, no special cases. This is a pacing gate, not a retry client; transport retries live elsewhere and I don't want the two fighting.
